name: Wild Bill Davis
description: American jazz pianist, organist, and arranger (1918–1995)
abstract: Wild Bill Davis (November 24, 1918 – August 17, 1995) was the stage name of American jazz pianist, organist, and arranger William Strethen Davis. He is best known for his pioneering jazz electric organ recordings and for his tenure with the Tympany Five, the backing group for Louis Jordan.

concerts.label: Newport Jazz Festival: Jazz on the Hammond
Kool Jazz Festival: Benny Carter and Illinois Jacquet

about_quotes.speaker: Arnett Cobb
about_quotes.evidence: I told you, Wild Bill Davis was in that band... Bill stayed with us two years.

about_quotes.speaker: Alvin Queen
about_quotes.evidence: And I spent many, many years as you know, with Horace Silver and George Benson and Wild Bill Davis and Tiny Grimes... And then Wild Bill Davis was one of my first gigs in Atlantic City, the Gracie Belmont Club
about_quotes.doc_title: Alvin Queen interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, August 23, 1997 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: John Budd
about_quotes.evidence: I knew most of them at the time when the band was Gene Schrader played the piano, Cutty Cutshaw played the trombone, Wild Bill Davis on the trumpet, and Walter Page on the bass and Eddie Condon on the guitar.
about_quotes.doc_title: John Budd interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 11, 1997 [transcript only]

about_quotes.speaker: Orrin Keepnews
about_quotes.evidence: I did find that a place like Nick’s in Greenwich Village where there were people like Pee Wee Russell, and Wild Bill Davis, were playing there. I had an exposure to jazz that way.
about_quotes.doc_title: Orrin Keepnews — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Maxine Sullivan
about_quotes.evidence: Budd Johnson was on his way through on tour and I worked one night with him, but with local musicians and so the Mosebacke is where he's situated now.

about_quotes.speaker: Jerry Dodgion
about_quotes.evidence: I’d met Oliver Nelson when were playing with Frank Sinatra with Red Norvo. I’d met him in Atlantic City. He was playing with Wild Bill Davis.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jerry Dodgion interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, March 9, 1996
